mn44:0.2: The Shorter Series of Questions and Answers
mn44:1.2: At one time, the _Bhagavā_ was dwelling at Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
mn44:1.3: Then Visākha the lay follower went to Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*. Having gone to Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*, he paid homage to her and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Visākha the lay follower said this to Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*:
mn44:10.1: “Is the noble eightfold path, madam, *saṅkhata* or *asaṅkhata*?”ti?
mn44:10.2: “The noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, is *saṅkhata*.”ti.
mn44:11.1: “Are the three aggregates included by the noble eightfold path, or is the noble eightfold path included by the three aggregates, madam?”ti?
mn44:11.2: “No, friend Visākha, the three aggregates are not included by the noble eightfold path; rather, the noble eightfold path is included by the three aggregates.
mn44:11.3: And, friend Visākha, right speech, right action, and right livelihood: these dhammas are included in the aggregate of ethical conduct.
mn44:11.4: And right effort, right *sati*, and right *samādhi*: these dhammas are included in the aggregate of *samādhi* [*samādhikkhandhe*].
mn44:11.5: And right view and right intention: these dhammas are included in the aggregate of discernment [*paññākkhandhe*].”ti.
mn44:12.1: “What then, madam, is *samādhi*? What dhammas are the *nimitta*s for *samādhi* [*samādhinimittā*]? What dhammas are the requisites for *samādhi* [*samādhiparikkhārā*]? What is the development [*bhāvanā*] of *samādhi* [*samādhibhāvanā*]?”ti?
mn44:12.2: “The singleness of mind [*cittassa ekaggatā*], friend Visākha, is *samādhi*;
mn44:12.4: the four right efforts are the requisites for *samādhi* [*samādhiparikkhārā*].
mn44:12.5: The practice, development [*bhāvanā*], and repeated cultivation of those very dhammas: this is the development [*bhāvanā*] of *samādhi* here [*samādhibhāvanā*].”ti.
mn44:14.1: “What then, madam, is body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*]? What is verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*]? What is mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”ti?
mn44:14.2: “In-breaths and out-breaths, friend Visākha, are body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*]; thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] are verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*]; *saññā* and *vedanā* are mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*].”ti.
mn44:15.1: “Why then, madam, are in-breaths and out-breaths body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*]? Why are thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*]? Why are *saññā* and *vedanā* mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”ti?
mn44:15.2: “In-breaths and out-breaths, friend Visākha, are bodily; these dhammas are bound up with the body, therefore in-breaths and out-breaths are body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*].
mn44:15.3: First, friend Visākha, having thought [*vitakka*] and examined [*vicāra*], one then breaks into speech; therefore thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*] are verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*].
mn44:15.4: *Saññā* and *vedanā* are mental; these dhammas are bound up with the mind [*cittappaṭibaddhā*], therefore *saññā* and *vedanā* are mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*].”ti.
mn44:16.1: “How then, madam, does the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpatti*] come to be?”ti?
mn44:16.2: “When a *bhikkhu* is attaining the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], friend Visākha, it does not occur to him:
mn44:16.3: ‘I will attain the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*],’ or ‘I am attaining the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*],’ or ‘I have attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*].’
mn44:16.4: Rather, his mind [*citta*] has already been developed [*bhāvitaṁ*] in such a way that it leads him to that state.”ti.
mn44:17.1: “When a *bhikkhu* is attaining the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], madam, which dhammas cease first: body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*], verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*], or mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”ti?
mn44:17.2: “When a *bhikkhu* is attaining the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], friend Visākha, verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*] ceases first, then body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*], then mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*].”ti.
mn44:18.1: “How then, madam, does emergence from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*] come to be?”ti?
mn44:18.2: “When a *bhikkhu* is emerging from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*], friend Visākha, it does not occur to him:
mn44:18.3: ‘I will emerge from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*],’ or ‘I am emerging from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*],’ or ‘I have emerged from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*].’
mn44:18.4: Rather, his mind [*citta*] has already been developed [*bhāvitaṁ*] in such a way that it leads him to that state.”ti.
mn44:19.1: “When a *bhikkhu* is emerging from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*], madam, which dhammas arise first: body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*], verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*], or mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*]?”ti?
mn44:19.2: “When a *bhikkhu* is emerging from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*], friend Visākha, mind-saṅkhāra [*cittasaṅkhāro*] arises first, then body-saṅkhāra [*kāyasaṅkhāro*], then verbal saṅkhāra [*vacīsaṅkhāro*].”ti.
mn44:2.2: What is called identity by the _Bhagavā_?”ti?
mn44:2.3: “These five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhā*], friend Visākha, are called identity by the _Bhagavā_,
mn44:2.4: that is: the *rūpa*-aggregate of clinging/feeding [*rūpupādānakkhandho*], the *vedanā*-aggregate of clinging/feeding [*vedanupādānakkhandho*], the *saññā*-aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saññupādānakkhandho*], the *saṅkhāra*-aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saṅkhārupādānakkhandho*], the consciousness [*viññāṇa*]-aggregate of clinging/feeding [*viññāṇupādānakkhandho*].
mn44:2.5: These five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], friend Visākha, are called identity by the _Bhagavā_.”ti.
mn44:2.6: “Good, madam.” Then Visākha the lay follower, having approved and agreed with what Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni* had said, asked Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni* a further question:
mn44:20.1: “How many contacts touch a *bhikkhu* who has emerged from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*], madam?”ti?
mn44:20.2: “A *bhikkhu* who has emerged from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*], friend Visākha, is touched by three contacts: the contact of emptiness, signless contact, and undirected contact.”ti.
mn44:21.1: “For a *bhikkhu* who has emerged from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*], madam, toward what does the mind [*citta*] incline, slope, and tend?”ti?
mn44:21.2: “For a *bhikkhu* who has emerged from the attainment of cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhasamāpattiyā*], friend Visākha, the mind [*citta*] inclines to seclusion [*vivekaninnaṁ*], slopes to seclusion [*vivekapoṇaṁ*], and tends to seclusion [*vivekapabbhāraṁ*].”ti.
mn44:23.1: “What then, madam, is *sukha* *vedanā*? What is *dukkha* *vedanā*? What is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhā vedanā*]?”ti?
mn44:23.2: “Whatever bodily or mental *sukha*, agreeable feeling, is felt—
mn44:23.4: Whatever bodily or mental *dukkha*, disagreeable feeling, is felt—
mn44:23.6: Whatever bodily or mental feeling that is neither agreeable nor disagreeable is felt—
mn44:24.1: “With respect to *sukha* *vedanā*, madam, what is *sukha* and what is *dukkha*? With respect to *dukkha* *vedanā*, what is *sukha* and what is *dukkha*? With respect to neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhā vedanā*], what is *sukha* and what is *dukkha*?”ti?
mn44:24.2: “*Sukha* *vedanā*, friend Visākha, is *sukha* in its persisting and *dukkha* in its change;
mn44:24.3: *dukkha* *vedanā* is *dukkha* in its persisting and *sukha* in its change;
mn44:24.4: neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhā vedanā*] is *sukha* with knowledge and *dukkha* with non-knowledge.”ti.
mn44:25.1: “What underlying tendency underlies *sukha* *vedanā*, madam? What underlying tendency underlies *dukkha* *vedanā*? What underlying tendency underlies neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*]?”ti?
mn44:25.2: “The underlying tendency to lust underlies *sukha* *vedanā*, friend Visākha; the underlying tendency to aversion underlies *dukkha* *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] underlies neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*].”ti.
mn44:26.1: “Does the underlying tendency to lust underlie every *sukha* *vedanā*, madam? Does the underlying tendency to aversion underlie every *dukkha* *vedanā*? Does the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] underlie every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*]?”ti?
mn44:26.2: “No, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to lust does not underlie every *sukha* *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to aversion does not underlie every *dukkha* *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] does not underlie every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*].”ti.
mn44:27.1: “What should be abandoned in relation to *sukha* *vedanā*, madam? What should be abandoned in relation to *dukkha* *vedanā*? What should be abandoned in relation to neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*]?”ti?
mn44:27.2: “In relation to *sukha* *vedanā*, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to lust should be abandoned; in relation to *dukkha* *vedanā*, the underlying tendency to aversion should be abandoned; in relation to neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*], the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] should be abandoned.”ti.
mn44:28.1: “Should the underlying tendency to lust be abandoned in relation to every *sukha* *vedanā*, madam? Should the underlying tendency to aversion be abandoned in relation to every *dukkha* *vedanā*? Should the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] be abandoned in relation to every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*]?”ti?
mn44:28.10: By means of that he abandons blindness [*avijjā*], and the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] does not underlie that.”ti.
mn44:28.2: “No, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to lust should not be abandoned in relation to every *sukha* *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to aversion should not be abandoned in relation to every *dukkha* *vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] should not be abandoned in relation to every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*].
mn44:28.3: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful dhammas, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn44:28.4: By means of that he abandons lust, and the underlying tendency to lust does not underlie that.
mn44:28.5: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu* reflects thus:
mn44:28.6: ‘When indeed will I enter and dwell in that dimension [*āyatana*] in which the noble ones now dwell, having entered that dimension [*āyatana*]?’
mn44:28.7: As he arouses longing for the unsurpassed liberations, distress arises in him conditioned by longing.
mn44:28.8: By means of that he abandons aversion, and the underlying tendency to aversion does not underlie that.
mn44:28.9: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu*, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier vanishing of joy and distress, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* [*adukkhamasukhaṁ*], with purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati*.
mn44:29.10: “The counterpart of knowledge, friend Visākha, is liberation.”ti.
mn44:29.11: “And what is the counterpart of liberation, madam?”ti?
mn44:29.12: “The counterpart of liberation, friend Visākha, is *nibbāna*.”ti.
mn44:29.13: “And what is the counterpart of *nibbāna*, madam?”ti?
mn44:29.14: “You have gone too far with the question, friend Visākha; you were not able to grasp the limit of questions.
mn44:29.15: For the spiritual life plunges into *nibbāna*, has *nibbāna* as its destination, and has *nibbāna* as its culmination.
mn44:29.16: And if you wish, friend Visākha, you may go to the _Bhagavā_ and ask him about this matter; and as the _Bhagavā_ answers you, so you should remember it.”ti.
mn44:29.3: “And what is the counterpart of *dukkha* *vedanā*, madam?”ti?
mn44:29.5: “And what is the counterpart of neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*], madam?”ti?
mn44:29.6: “The counterpart of neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā* [*adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya*], friend Visākha, is blindness [*avijjā*].”ti.
mn44:29.7: “And what is the counterpart of blindness [*avijjā*], madam?”ti?
mn44:29.8: “The counterpart of blindness [*avijjā*], friend Visākha, is knowledge.”ti.
mn44:29.9: “And what is the counterpart of knowledge, madam?”ti?
mn44:3.1: “‘Origin of identity, origin of identity,’ they say, madam.
mn44:3.2: What is called the origin of identity by the _Bhagavā_?”ti?
mn44:3.3: “That *taṇhā*, friend Visākha, which leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting now here, now there, that is:—
mn44:3.5: this, friend Visākha, is called the origin of identity by the _Bhagavā_.”ti.
mn44:30.1: Then Visākha the lay follower, having approved and agreed with what Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni* had said, rose from his seat, paid homage to Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*, and, keeping her on his right, went to the _Bhagavā_. Having gone to the _Bhagavā_, he paid homage to him and sat down to one side.
mn44:30.2: Sitting to one side, Visākha the lay follower reported to the _Bhagavā_ the entire conversation he had had with Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*.
mn44:30.3: When this was said, the _Bhagavā_ said this to Visākha the lay follower:
mn44:30.4: “Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*, Visākha, is wise. Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*, Visākha, is of great discernment [*mahāpaññā*].
mn44:30.5: Even if you had asked me about this matter, Visākha, I too would answer it in exactly the same way as it was answered by Dhammadinnā the *bhikkhuni*.
mn44:30.6: This is precisely the meaning of it. And that is how you should remember it.”ti.
mn44:30.8: Satisfied, Visākha the lay follower approved of what the _Bhagavā_ said.
mn44:30.9: The Shorter Series of Questions and Answers is finished, the fourth.
mn44:4.1: “‘Cessation of identity, cessation of identity,’ they say, madam.
mn44:4.2: What is called the cessation of identity by the _Bhagavā_?”ti?
mn44:4.3: “The remainderless fading away and cessation of that very *taṇhā*—giving up, relinquishment, release, non-attachment;
mn44:4.4: this, friend Visākha, is called the cessation of identity by the _Bhagavā_.”ti.
mn44:5.1: “‘The practice leading to the cessation of identity, the practice leading to the cessation of identity,’ they say, madam.
mn44:5.2: What is called the practice leading to the cessation of identity by the _Bhagavā_?”ti?
mn44:5.3: “This very noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, is called the practice leading to the cessation of identity by the _Bhagavā_, that is:—
mn44:5.4: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.”ti.
mn44:6.1: “Is that very clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] those five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], or is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] something apart from the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcahupādānakkhandhehi*], madam?”ti?
mn44:6.2: “No, friend Visākha, that very clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] is not those five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], nor is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] something apart from the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcahupādānakkhandhehi*].
mn44:6.3: Whatever desire and lust there is in regard to the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcasu upādānakkhandhesu*], that is the clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] there.”ti.
mn44:7.1: “How then, madam, does identity view come to be?”ti?
mn44:7.2: “Here, friend Visākha, an unlearned ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, is unskilled in the noble *dhamma*, untrained in the noble *dhamma*; who has not seen good persons, is unskilled in the *dhamma* of good persons, untrained in the *dhamma* of good persons,
mn44:7.3: regards *rūpa* as self, or self as possessing *rūpa*, or *rūpa* in self, or self in *rūpa*.
mn44:7.7: he regards consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as self, or self as possessing consciousness [*viññāṇa*], or consciousness [*viññāṇa*] in self, or self in consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn44:7.8: That is how identity view comes to be, friend Visākha.”ti.
mn44:8.1: “How then, madam, does identity view not come to be?”ti?
mn44:8.2: “Here, friend Visākha, a learned noble disciple, who has seen the noble ones, is skilled in the noble *dhamma*, well trained in the noble *dhamma*; who has seen good persons, is skilled in the *dhamma* of good persons, well trained in the *dhamma* of good persons,
mn44:8.3: does not regard *rūpa* as self, or self as possessing *rūpa*, or *rūpa* in self, or self in *rūpa*.
mn44:8.4: He does not regard *vedanā* as self …
mn44:8.7: he does not regard consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as self, or self as possessing consciousness [*viññāṇa*], or consciousness [*viññāṇa*] in self, or self in consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn44:8.8: That is how identity view does not come to be, friend Visākha.”ti.
mn44:9.1: “What then, madam, is the noble eightfold path?”ti?
mn44:9.2: “This very noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, that is:—
mn44:9.3: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.”ti.
